metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC3 to explicitly require at least one test per named reader: `signing._record_for_kind`, `validate.py:signature_findings`, and `txn.py:_signature_gate`, not just 'the consumers' generically.
findings.finding: AC3 says tests cover 'the consumers resolving via the map' but does not enumerate all three named mirror readers (signing._record_for_kind, validate.py:signature_findings, txn.py:_signature_gate). An executor could write a single consumer test and satisfy the AC as written, leaving two readers unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('Verified by unit tests … covering … the consumers resolving via the map')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the consumers resolving via the map' in AC3 with: 'each of the three named readers (signing._record_for_kind, validate.py:signature_findings, txn.py:_signature_gate) resolving correctly via most_recent_attestation on a mirror-less snapshot'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact target patch version for the rollback flag in the plan (e.g., '0.7.2') so docs/migrations.md, CHANGELOG, and any release tag all use the same identifier.
findings.finding: AC2 states the rollback target as 'that flag on the 0.7.x line (introduced after 0.7.1)' without pinning an exact version (e.g., 0.7.2). An executor must decide the version number and may document it inconsistently across docs/migrations.md and the changelog.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 ('Named rollback target: that flag on the 0.7.x line (introduced after 0.7.1)')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'introduced after 0.7.1' with the concrete next patch version being cut (e.g., 'introduced in 0.7.2') so the rollback target is unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability: emit a structured log (warn level) in `most_recent_attestation` when the fallback branch fires, and a debug/info log in `compact.py` when the `signature` key is stripped, so operators can verify migration progress and detect anomalous fallback cases.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability instrumentation (log line, metric, or trace) for two new silent failure/fallback points: (1) when `most_recent_attestation` takes the fallback branch (`state['signature']` used because map is absent/empty — which on a post-upgrade snapshot would be anomalous), and (2) when compact.py actually strips the `signature` key during compaction. Operators will have no signal that the migration is progressing or that an unexpected fallback is occurring.
findings.suggested_fix: In `most_recent_attestation`, add `logger.warning('most_recent_attestation: attestations map absent/empty, falling back to legacy state[signature]', extra={'snapshot_id': ...})`. In `compact.py`'s strip path, add `logger.info('compact: stripped legacy signature mirror from snapshot', extra={'snapshot_id': ...})`. Expose a counter metric (e.g. `rebar_legacy_signature_fallback_total`) if a metrics framework is available.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (in docs/migrations.md and the AC) the maximum expected latency between flag flip and mirror re-emission, and provide a runbook step (e.g., `rebar compact --fsck-rebuild`) to force immediate re-emission when urgent rollback is required.
findings.finding: The rollback path (flip `emit_legacy_signature_mirror = true`) causes the mirror to be re-emitted only on the NEXT compaction run. Already-written mirror-less SNAPSHOTs are not retroactively repaired. The plan does not specify the maximum time between flag flip and compaction re-emission, nor does it provide a procedure (e.g., trigger fsck-rebuild immediately) to close this window when a pre-attestations reader must urgently be reinstated.
findings.location: Rollback + named target version (AC line 2); What — step 3 (fsck-rebuild path mention)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to docs/migrations.md: 'After setting `REBAR_COMPACT_EMIT_LEGACY_SIGNATURE_MIRROR=true`, trigger an immediate compaction via `rebar compact --force` (or fsck-rebuild) to repair all live snapshots before reinstating any pre-attestations readers. Normal scheduled compaction occurs every N minutes; do not wait for it in an urgent rollback.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit statement (in AC or docs/migrations.md) either confirming there are no out-of-tree consumers of `state['signature']` (scripts, dashboards, external services) or listing them and their migration status as part of the readiness gate.
findings.finding: The plan migrates all three *in-tree* readers of `state['signature']` but does not acknowledge whether any *out-of-tree* consumers (e.g., monitoring dashboards, audit scripts, external reconcile hosts, or other services that parse snapshot files directly) read the legacy `signature` field. For the 'contract' phase to be complete, all consumers — not just Python-module readers — must be confirmed migrated or confirmed non-existent before the mirror is dropped.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'operator-attested' AC, expand the evidence clause to include: 'No out-of-tree tooling, monitoring queries, or external services read state[\'signature\'] from snapshot files — confirmed by [grep of ops scripts / audit of dashboards / statement that snapshots are not externally parsed].'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `most_recent_attestation` is genuinely needed as a separate public helper, or whether updating `_record_for_kind`'s kind=None branch in-place (plus having `signature_findings` and `_signature_gate` call `_record_for_kind(state, None)`) achieves the same migration with fewer moving parts; if the new helper is kept, document why it cannot simply replace or extend `_record_for_kind`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new public helper `signing.most_recent_attestation(state)` when the existing private function `signing._record_for_kind(state, None)` already provides the extraction seam for the same three readers. Updating `_record_for_kind`'s kind=None branch to draw from `state['attestations']` (with `state['signature']` fallback) and letting `signature_findings` and `_signature_gate` call `_record_for_kind(state, None)` would achieve the same migration without adding a second indirection layer — Rule-of-Three is at exactly the threshold, and one of the three 'sites' is the existing private function itself.
findings.location: What section, step 1 ('ADD a NEW helper `signing.most_recent_attestation(state)`'); step 2 (migrate `signing._record_for_kind` kind=None path, `validate.py:signature_findings`, `txn.py:_signature_gate`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the `most_recent_attestation` spec (and in the `signature_findings` migration note) that the `state['signature']` fallback only fires for pre-drop SNAPSHOTs (where the key persists in compiled_state); post-drop SNAPSHOTs rely entirely on the `attestations` map, and the helper should never return stale data from an absent key when the map is populated.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `_record_for_kind` serves as the sole 'kind=None verify-latest path' that reads the mirror, but the codebase also contains a direct `state.get('signature')` read in `validate.py:signature_findings` (line 114) that the plan lists separately as a third reader. However, `validate.py:signature_findings` does NOT call `_record_for_kind` — it calls `reduce_ticket` and reads `state.get('signature')` directly. After the mirror is stripped from new SNAPSHOTs, `reduce_ticket` on a post-drop compacted ticket will return state WITHOUT a `signature` key (process_snapshot restores compiled_state verbatim, and compiled_state will lack `signature`). The plan correctly identifies `signature_findings` as a reader to migrate, but fails to note that the fold-in at `_processors.py:655-660` only populates `attestations` when `attestations` is ABSENT from compiled_state — a post-drop SNAPSHOT carries `attestations` already, so the fold-in is skipped, and `state['signature']` is never populated for such tickets. This means `signature_findings` after the drop will see `record = None` for all post-drop-compacted tickets and silently stop verifying them — even before step 2's migration is applied. The plan's ordering guarantee ('no reader ever sees a stripped mirror') rests on step 2 completing before step 3, but if `signature_findings` is migrated to call `most_recent_attestation`, that helper's fallback to `state['signature']` also won't help for post-drop SNAPSHOTs. The net behavior (using `most_recent_attestation`) is correct, but the plan's explanation of WHY the fallback is safe is imprecise: the fallback to `state['signature']` only applies to pre-drop SNAPSHOTs that still carry the key in compiled_state; post-drop SNAPSHOTs rely entirely on the `attestations` map path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `emit_legacy_signature_mirror` bool coercion to the `compact` section's coercion schema in `_config_schema.py` (following the `_as_bool` pattern at line 445), and add the flag to `docs/config.md` / `docs/env-vars.md` in Scope.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the `emit_legacy_signature_mirror` config flag will live under `compact` (i.e., `compact.emit_legacy_signature_mirror`) with default `false`, and names the env var `REBAR_COMPACT_EMIT_LEGACY_SIGNATURE_MIRROR`. The existing `CompactConfig` dataclass (confirmed at `_config_schema.py:321-329`) has only `threshold` and `COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS` fields, and the config coercion schema at `_config_schema.py` uses a `_SECTION_COERCION` dict for field-level coercers. The plan never specifies adding the field to `_SECTION_COERCION` / the coercion schema, nor does it mention updating `docs/config.md` or `docs/env-vars.md` to document the new flag. Since `emit_legacy_signature_mirror` is a boolean, it needs `_as_bool` coercion wired in (as `require_signature_for_close` has at `_config_schema.py:445`), and an unknown key in `compact` section would trigger a warning (line 200-205). This wiring is in scope but is not called out as a task.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_config_schema.py`, add `emit_legacy_signature_mirror: bool = False` to `CompactConfig` and add `'emit_legacy_signature_mirror': lambda v, k: _as_bool(v, k)` to the compact section's coercion dict. Add `docs/config.md` and `docs/env-vars.md` to Scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `docs/migrations.md` is the appropriate home for the 'Legacy signature-mirror retirement' one-way-door section (given it currently documents only School B convergent migrations), or explicitly call out adding a new section header and explaining why it fits there (or choose `docs/release-notes.md` / a new `docs/format-changes.md`).
findings.finding: The plan names `docs/migrations.md` as the location for the 'Legacy signature-mirror retirement' section (referenced in two ACs). Probing the file confirms it exists but currently documents only the ensure-registry (School B convergent migrations) with no version-ledger sections and no mention of signature or attestation retirement. The plan's AC says 'Documented in docs/migrations.md "Legacy signature-mirror retirement"' — this section must be NEW content. However, the existing `docs/migrations.md` explicitly states 'There is no ordered version ledger' and is structured around the ensure-registry. A 'retirement' doc for a one-way-door format change may not fit naturally in this file's School B framing, risking the documentation landing in an incoherent location or being skipped.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` to the Scope section (even if no edits are needed) with a note that step 4's 'keep' directive means the file is in-scope-for-review but no code changes are required.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` is explicitly referenced in the What section (step 4: 'Keep the read-side fold-in (`_processors.py`)') but is absent from the Scope section, creating a documentation inconsistency. The file contains both the mirror WRITE (`process_signature` line 493: `state['signature'] = record`) and the fourth direct mirror READ (`process_snapshot` line 656: `state.get('signature')`). Omitting it from Scope means reviewers and implementers have no clear directive about what (if anything) in this file is in or out of scope for this ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` to the Scope section with annotation: '(no edits — step 4 explicitly preserves the existing fold-in read and mirror write)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct step 2's 'all three' count to 'all four in-tree mirror readers' (or to 'the three readers being migrated, plus `_processors.py:process_snapshot` which step 4 intentionally preserves') to accurately reflect the full reader inventory.
findings.finding: Step 2 claims to enumerate 'all three in-tree mirror readers' of `state['signature']`, but `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:656` (`process_snapshot`) is a fourth direct `state.get('signature')` reader that is not counted in step 2's list of three. Step 4 says to 'keep' the `_processors.py` fold-in, which is the right decision, but the '3' count in step 2 is factually incorrect. An implementer following step 2 as a complete checklist would not know to confirm that `_processors.py:656` does or does not need the `most_recent_attestation` treatment.
findings.suggested_fix: Change step 2's opening to: 'Migrate the three in-tree mirror readers that need map-sourced replacements (a fourth, `_processors.py:process_snapshot`, is intentionally kept — see step 4): ...'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale (1–2 sentences) to the plan explaining WHY the write-side strip lives in `compact.py` rather than in `process_signature` (e.g., 'stripping in compaction confines the change to persisted SNAPSHOTs, preserves live-state mirror compatibility during the transition window, and bounds blast radius to the compaction path alone').
findings.finding: The plan offers no positive rationale for why the chosen mechanism (new helper + reader migration + compaction-side flag) is the right approach — its 'Why' section explains business motivation for the contract phase but not the technical design choice.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section or a new 'Design rationale' sentence: 'The mirror is stripped at compaction time rather than in the reducer so that live in-memory state during event replay is unchanged, preserving compatibility for any code path that reduces a ticket without subsequently snapshotting it, and keeping the blast radius limited to the SNAPSHOT write path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `most_recent_attestation` spec to acknowledge that 'greatest `signed_at`' is the INTENDED semantics (not a replica of the mirror's event-sort LWW), document any known divergence from the legacy mirror, and confirm all three consuming sites (`_record_for_kind`, `signature_findings`, `_signature_gate`) are correct under the new semantics rather than the legacy ones.
findings.finding: `most_recent_attestation`'s specified semantics (max `signed_at` from the attestations map) do not precisely reproduce the legacy `state['signature']` mirror's behavior (last SIGNATURE *event* processed by HLC file sort), and the plan asserts they do — this is a spec imprecision that could cause silent behavioral divergence for multi-kind tickets where event-file sort order and `signed_at` values disagree.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify and verify the `most_recent_attestation()` selection semantics: either document that `signed_at`-order intentionally replaces HLC-replay-order (and justify the security equivalence), or change the implementation to use event timestamp (HLC) rather than `signed_at` to guarantee byte-identical behavior with the legacy mirror's last-writer-wins.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `most_recent_attestation(state)` 'reproduces the mirror's last signature of any kind' with 'ties → last processed, matching last-writer-wins,' but the legacy mirror uses deterministic HLC event replay order (filename-sort) while the proposed helper selects by `signed_at` (wall-clock nanoseconds from `time.time_ns()`). These orderings can diverge under clock skew or out-of-order event delivery, meaning the helper may select a different record than the legacy mirror in edge cases — an unverified behavioral-equivalence claim on a security-sensitive code path.
findings.location: What section, step 1 ('greatest `signed_at` … matching the legacy mirror's last-writer-wins'); signing.py:_record_for_kind; _commands/txn.py:_signature_gate (line 362)
findings.suggested_fix: Use the event's HLC timestamp (`record.get('signed_at') or 0` falling back to `event.get('timestamp')`) as the sort key — which is what `process_signature` already stores in the record as `signed_at: data.get('signed_at') or event.get('timestamp')` — but note that in `compiled_state` the event timestamp is not available; instead, document explicitly that the helper returns `max(attestations.values(), key=lambda r: r.get('signed_at') or 0)` and add a comment acknowledging that `signed_at` is the best available proxy (wall-clock, not HLC) and that it can differ from replay order. Add a unit test exercising the clock-skew case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the post-rollback schema state for already-stripped SNAPSHOTs: either (a) document that re-enabling the flag must be followed by a `rebar compact-all` sweep (add this to the rollback runbook in docs/migrations.md and verify it in the rollback unit test), or (b) extend the `process_snapshot` fold-in to also synthesize `state['signature']` from `attestations` when `signature` is absent, so stripped SNAPSHOTs self-heal on first read without re-compaction.
findings.finding: The rollback mechanism (re-enabling `emit_legacy_signature_mirror`) re-emits `signature` only on the NEXT compaction of each ticket. SNAPSHOTs already written without `signature` are NOT retroactively repaired by the flag flip, and the fold-in logic in `process_snapshot` does NOT restore `signature` from `attestations` (it only folds in the opposite direction). A rolled-back old binary reading an already-stripped SNAPSHOT will observe `state['signature'] = None`, causing the still-unpatched `_signature_gate` (txn.py:362) and `validate.py` `signature_findings` to fail or produce incorrect results. The plan asserts rollback exits successfully but does not assert the schema STATE of already-stripped snapshots after rollback, nor provide a fleet-wide re-compaction step to close this window.
findings.location: AC: Rollback + named target version / Step 3 (compact.py strip) / docs/migrations.md
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the `process_snapshot` fold-in (already at ~line 655 of `_processors.py`) with a second clause: after the existing `if 'attestations' not in compiled_state:` block, add `if 'signature' not in compiled_state and isinstance(state.get('attestations'), dict): state['signature'] = most_recent_attestation(state)`. This makes every stripped SNAPSHOT self-heal on read (idempotent, no re-compaction needed), closing the rollback window without requiring a fleet sweep.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the full `compact.emit_legacy_signature_mirror` schema extension: add the `bool` field to `CompactConfig`, the `_as_bool` lambda to `_SECTIONS['compact']`, and (if needed) an env-var alias entry — all three changes must be named in the plan.
findings.finding: The new `compact.emit_legacy_signature_mirror` config flag is referenced in both the 'What' section and rollback AC, but its schema vocabulary — the `CompactConfig` dataclass field, the `_SECTIONS['compact']` coercion lambda, and the env-var alias registration — is never specified anywhere in the plan. The existing `CompactConfig` (line 321) has only `threshold` and `COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS`; `_SECTIONS['compact']` (line 467–470) has only those two coercers. An implementer has no specified type (`bool`?), no coercer (`_as_bool`?), and no env-var alias entry, leaving the schema extension underspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope/What section, add: '`_config_schema.py`: add `emit_legacy_signature_mirror: bool = False` to `CompactConfig`; add `"emit_legacy_signature_mirror": lambda v, k: _as_bool(v, k)` to `_SECTIONS["compact"]`; register `("compact", "EMIT_LEGACY_SIGNATURE_MIRROR"): "REBAR_COMPACT_EMIT_LEGACY_SIGNATURE_MIRROR"` in the env-var alias table in `config.py`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the full comparison protocol for `most_recent_attestation`: primary key (`signed_at` as a numeric timestamp), secondary fallback when `signed_at` is `None` (treat as 0 or use `event.timestamp`), and tie-breaking rule (e.g., last by kind string, matching dict-insertion order behavior).
findings.finding: The `most_recent_attestation(state)` tie-breaking rule — 'ties → last processed, matching the legacy mirror's last-writer-wins' — is a decision protocol referenced but never co-located with or derivable from the `attestations` schema. `state['attestations']` is a plain `dict` keyed by kind (not an ordered sequence), so 'last processed' has no deterministic Python-dict meaning. The plan never specifies the sort key (e.g., compare `signed_at` timestamps, fall back to event `timestamp` when `signed_at` is `None`, then break remaining ties by dict insertion order / kind string). Without this protocol, two implementers will write different functions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the migration strategy for `_record_for_kind(kind=None)`: specify explicitly whether its body is updated to delegate to `most_recent_attestation`, or whether its call-sites are switched to call `most_recent_attestation` directly, and update the docstring to match.
findings.finding: The plan says 'Migrate … `signing._record_for_kind` (kind=None verify-latest path)' to call `most_recent_attestation`, but `_record_for_kind`'s existing docstring explicitly contracts that `kind=None` 'returns the legacy most-recent `state[\'signature\']` mirror (EXACT pre-attestations behavior)'. The plan never specifies whether `_record_for_kind` is (a) refactored in-place to delegate to `most_recent_attestation`, (b) deprecated and call-sites switched to call `most_recent_attestation` directly, or (c) kept as-is with its docstring updated. This is an instruction-locality gap: the migration instruction in 'What §2' contradicts the function's own documented contract without resolving the conflict.

The plan's named migration target `signing.most_recent_attestation()` does not exist in the codebase and is not specified anywhere in the plan; implementers have no contract for what this function should do (especially for `kind=None`, which must now scan `state['attestations']` by `signed_at` to reproduce the legacy LWW behavior).
findings.location: What section / 'Migrate every in-tree reader of the legacy mirror to signing.most_recent_attestation()'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: '`signing.most_recent_attestation(state)` returns `max(state["attestations"].values(), key=lambda r: r.get("signed_at") or 0, default=None)` when `attestations` is present and non-empty, else falls back to `state.get("signature")` (the old mirror, present in pre-contract SNAPSHOTs). `_record_for_kind(state, None)` delegates to this helper.'

findings.finding: `_signature_gate` in `txn.py` reads `state.get('signature')` directly (line 362); after `compact.py` strips the mirror under the default flag, a freshly-compacted ticket that holds a valid attestation in `state['attestations']` but no `state['signature']` will cause `_signature_gate` to return `unsigned` and block closure — breaking the `require_signature_for_close` gate for every ticket that has been compacted after the contract drop.

The migration of `validate.py:signature_findings` from `state.get('signature')` to a map-sourced read is mentioned in the plan but its new semantics are unspecified: the current code verifies the single most-recent record (one verdict per ticket); after the mirror is dropped, the check must iterate `state['attestations']` — but should it flag a MISMATCH on any kind, all kinds, or only the most-recent kind? This ambiguity risks either a silent no-op (loop body skipped because `record = state.get('signature')` returns None) or over-flagging (foreign-key verdicts for kinds the local environment didn't sign).

`signing.most_recent_attestation()` is referenced as the migration target for all legacy `state['signature']` readers, but this function does not exist in `signing.py` and its contract (argument signature, return type, fallback when `attestations` map is absent) is never defined anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Plan § What: 'Migrate every in-tree reader of the legacy mirror to `signing.most_recent_attestation()`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan's 'What' section: '`signing.most_recent_attestation(state: dict) -> dict | None` — returns the attestation record with the highest `signed_at` across all kinds in `state["attestations"]`, or `None` when the map is absent/empty. No fallback to `state["signature"]` (mirror-independent). Replaces the `_record_for_kind(state, None)` call in `verify_signature` and the `state.get("signature")` calls in `validate.signature_findings` and `txn._signature_gate`.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Migrate every in-tree reader of the legacy mirror to signing.most_recent_attestation()' — but signing.most_recent_attestation() does not exist anywhere in the codebase. A repo-wide grep returns zero hits. signing.py (read in full) has no such function. The named migration target is fictional; the committed migration work rests on a non-existent API.
findings.location: What section — 'Migrate every in-tree reader of the legacy mirror to signing.most_recent_attestation()'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'signing.most_recent_attestation()' throughout the plan with 'signing._record_for_kind (rewritten to fall back to state["attestations"] for kind=None)' and add an explicit note that _record_for_kind's kind=None branch is the only change needed — no new public function is introduced.

findings.finding: The post-rollback schema state is not fully specified: setting `emit_legacy_signature_mirror = true` re-enables mirror emission on future compactions, but SNAPSHOTs already written without `signature` remain mirror-less until each ticket is individually re-compacted. Old code restored to service during rollback will read `state['signature'] = None` from those SNAPSHOTs, which is exactly the broken state the rollback is meant to avoid.
findings.location: AC: Rollback + named target version; 'What' section: compact.py strip
findings.suggested_fix: In docs/migrations.md add a 'Rollback procedure' subsection: '1. Set compact.emit_legacy_signature_mirror = true. 2. Run rebar compact-all to re-mint the mirror on every existing SNAPSHOT. 3. Verify with rebar fsck that no mirror-less SNAPSHOTs remain. 4. Only then re-activate any pre-attestations binary.' Add a unit test that writes a mirror-less SNAPSHOT, sets the flag, runs compaction, and asserts the resulting SNAPSHOT contains signature.

findings.finding: The 'What' section names `signing.most_recent_attestation()` as the map-sourced replacement API that every in-tree legacy mirror reader is migrated to, but this function does not exist anywhere in the codebase. The actual internal helper is `_record_for_kind` and the public surface is `verify_signature`. It is unresolved whether `most_recent_attestation()` is (a) a new function to be authored as part of this ticket, or (b) a placeholder name for an existing symbol. If (a), the plan omits it from Scope and ACs; if (b), implementers will search for a non-existent API.
findings.location: What section — 'Migrate every in-tree reader of the legacy mirror to `signing.most_recent_attestation()`'
findings.suggested_fix: If a new helper is intended, add `signing.most_recent_attestation(state)` (or similar) to Scope and define its contract: 'returns the most-recent signature record from `state["attestations"]` by `signed_at` descending, falling back to `state["signature"]` only when the map is absent — so it works on both old and new SNAPSHOTs during the transition.' Also add `validate.py:signature_findings` to the What migration list.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
